front: Chapter 1 Introduction > What is an OS?
back: A program that acts as an intermediary between user and hardware

front: What is virtualization?
back: A method that allows operating systems to run applications within other OS'es

front: Chapter 4 Names and The Environment > Operations on Environments > When are changes in the environment produced?
back: At entry and exit from a block

front: Chapter 4 Names and The Environment > Scope Rules > Where is a declaration local to a block visible?
back: That block and all the blocks nested within it

front: Chapter 4 Names and The Environment > Scope Rules > Static Scope > What is the difference between static and lexical scope?
back: They are two words for the same thing

front: Chapter 5 Memory Management > Techniques for Memory Management > Is memory management a function of a compiler or interpreter associated with an abstract machine?
back: Interpreter

front: Chapter 5 Memory Management > Techniques for Memory Management > Which functionality manages the allocation of memory for programs and for data?
back: Interpreter

front: Chapter 5 Memory Management > Static Memory Management > When is static memory management performed and by what?
back: By the compiler before execution starts

front: Chapter 5 Memory Management > Static Memory Management > Where does statically allocated memory objects reside?
back: In a fixed zone of memory which is determined by the compiler, and they remain there for the entire durectino of the program's execution.

front: Chapter 5 Memory Management > Static Memory Management > Are constants, object code and global variables usually statically or dynamically allocated?
back: Statically

front: Chapter 5 Memory Management > Dynamic Memory Management Using Stacks > What is the LIFO scheme?
back: Last in first out.

front: Chapter 5 Memory Management > Dynamic Memory Management Using Stacks > A block A is entered, then a block B is entered, with the LIFO scheme, which block do we leave first?
Chapter 5 Memory Management > Dynamic Memory Management Using Stacks > A stack uses which scheme, LIFO, FIFO or FILO?
back: LIFO

front: Chapter 5 Memory Management > Dynamic Memory Management Using Stacks > What is the memory space allocated on the stack, dedicated to an in-line block or to an activation of a procedure called?
back: The activation record or frame.

front: Chapter 5 Memory Management > Dynamic Memory Management Using Stacks > What does the structure of a generic activation record for an in-line block hold?
back: Dynamic chain pointer, local variables and intermediate results

front: Chapter 5 Memory Management > Dynamic Memory Management Using Stacks > What are intermediate results in an in-line block of an activation record?
back: Results which are only needed for something else. For example b = (a + x) / (x + y), a+x and x+y will be calculated and stored before they are divided (depending on the compiler).

front: Chapter 5 Memory Management > Dynamic Memory Management Using Stacks > What does the dynamic chain pointer store? (recall: in-line block of a generic activation record)
back: A pointer to the previous activation record on the stack (or to the last activation record created).

front: Chapter 5 Memory Management > Dynamic Memory Management Using Stacks > What is a procedure?
back: An independent code module that fulfills some concrete task and is referenced within a larger body of source code

front: Chapter 5 Memory Management > Dynamic Memory Management Using Stacks > What is the difference in an activation record for a procedure and for a function?
back: For functions the activation record must also keep tabs on the memory location in which the function stores its return value.

front: Chapter 5 Memory Management > Dynamic Memory Management Using Stacks > How is the runtime management of the system stack implemented?
back: By code fragments which the compiler or interpreter inserts immediately before and after the call to a procedure or before the start and after the end of a block

front: 06 Control Structures > Review Questions > Why do we need memory?
back: To store the state of the program

front: 06 Control Structures > Review Questions > What kinds of data structures are we using in memory management ?
back: Stack and the heap

front: 06 Control Structures > Review Questions > What does it mean that we are using a stack for memory purposes?
back: We use them to push activation records or frames.

front: 06 Control Structures > Review Questions > Why do we need a stack for dynamic memory, instead of just dumping everything in memory?
back: We need it for recursion. With recursion we cannot allocate all possible blocks, but we can with a stack.

front: 06 Control Structures > Review Questions > How do we implement static scoping?
back: We are using the static chain pointer. The compiler adds this pointer to the previous "father" block, which contains the known local variable.

front: 06 Control Structures > Expressions > What is an expression?
back: A syntactic entity whose evaluation yields a value or does not end, in which case the expression is undefined. There are three main notations, infix, prefix and postfix.

front: 06 Control Structures > Commands > What are side effects?
back: Changing the state of computation without returning a value

front: 06 Control Structures > Commands > When was if then else introduced?
back: In ALGOL 60

front: 07 Control Abstraction > Control Abstraction > What are the two main ways that parameters are passed?
back: By value and by reference

front: Chapter 1 > Operating-system operations > Does the bootstrap program tend to be simple or complex?
back: Simple

front: Chapter 1 > Operating-system operations > Where is the bootstrap program usually stored?
back: In the computer hardware in firmware

front: Chapter 1 > Operating-system operations > What loads the kernel into memory?
back: The bootstrap program

front: Chapter 1 > Operating-system operations > Traps (or exceptions) are another form of interrupts, and is software generated. Why are they caused?
back: Because of errors or by a specific request from a user program that an operating-system service be performed by executing a special operation called a system call.

front: Chapter 1 > Operating-system operations > Multiprogramming and Multitasking > What does multiprogramming do?
back: Increases CPU utilization, as well as keeping users satisfied by organizing programs so that the CPU always has one to execute.

front: Chapter 1 > Operating-system operations > Multiprogramming and Multitasking > What are programs in execution called in a multiprogrammed system?
back: A process.

front: Week 6 > What is the main difficulty that a programmer must overcome in writing an operating system for a real-time environment?
back: Keeping the operating system within the fixed time constraints of a real-time system.

front: Week 6 > How does the distinction between kernel mode and user mode function as a rudimentary form of protection (security)?
back: It provides a form of security, in that certain instructions can not be executed unless in kernel mode. Similarly, hardware devices can only be accessed when the program is in kernel mode.

front: Week 6 > Some CPUs provide for more than two modes of operation. What are two possible uses of these multiple modes?
back: Different kinds of user modes. Specific kernel modes, for example if you want a mode to only be able to use USB

front: Week 6 > Give two reasons why caches are useful. What problems do they solve? What problems do they cause? If a cache can be made as large as the device for which it is caching (for instance, a cache as large as a disk), why not make it that large and eliminate the device?
back: Caches are useful when two or more components need to exchange data, and the components perform transfers at differing speeds.

front: Week 6 > How do clustered systems differ from multiprocessor systems? What is required for two machines belonging to a cluster to cooperate to provide a highly available service?
back: Clustered systems use messages to communicate, while processors in a multiprocessor system could communicate using shared memory

front: 07 Control Abstraction > Review from last > Why do we care about expressions?
back: Because we want to compute stuff

front: 06 Control Structures > Commands > Why do we not use goto anymore?
back: It makes the code harder to read

front: 08 Types > Review > How many ways of passing parameters are there?
back: 6, Call by value, reference, result, result-value, constant and name

front: 08 Types > Review > What are the pros of call by value?
back: You don't have to worry about side-effects.

front: 08 Types > Review > What are the pro of call by reference?
back: You don't have to copy anything

front: 08 Types > Review > What is the particularity of call by constant?
back: It's read only, otherwise it works like call by reference.

front: 08 Types > Finally and Return in Java > What is the idea of finally?
back: To have a block that will be executed no matter what in a try block.

front: 08 Types > Review > What is the difference between an exception and an error?
back: An Error "indicates serious problems that a reasonable application should not try to catch." An Exception "indicates conditions that a reasonable application might want to catch."

front: 08 Types > Types > What is a type?
back: A collection of values (homogeneous and effectively represented (can be visualized)) equipped with a set of operations to manipulate these values.

front: 08 Types > Types > What defines what is a type and what is not a type?
back: The programming language.

front: 08 Types > Types > How many categories of types are there?
back: Two, scalar types (or simple types) and composite types.

front: 08 Types > Types > What is a scalar type?
back: A type where values are not composed of aggregations of other values (e.g. boolean, chars).

front: 08 Types > Types > What are composite types?
back: Types obtained by combining other types with appropriate constructors (e.g. string, pointer, struct).

front: 08 Types > Types > When were enumerations introduced?
back: In Pascal

front: 08 Types > Types > Are enumerations ordered or unordered?
back: Ordered

front: 08 Types > Types > When were intervals (subranges) introduced?
back: In Pascal

front: 08 Types > Types > Type LessThan = 0..9 is an example of which type?
back: Intervals

front: 08 Types > Type equivalence > When are two types equivalent?
back: If each object of type T1 is also an object of type T2 and vice versa. T1 is compatible with T2 if an object of T1 can appear where a T2 would be expected.

front: 08 Types > Type equivalence > What is type equivalence by name?
back: Two types are equivalent if they have the same name. Used in Pascal, Ada and Java

front: 07 Control Abstraction > Review from last > Can you do lazy evaluation if you evaluate right-to-left?
back: No

front: 07 Control Abstraction > Review from last > Why do we care about tail recursion?
back: It's faster

front: 07 Control Abstraction > Control Abstraction > You can call by value and by reference, but what else?
back: Constant, Result and value-result

front: 07 Control Abstraction > Control Abstraction > What is call by result?
back: Opposite of value. The value of the result is returned and modified, but is not a reference. foo(result y) {y + 1}, then y would be y + 1 from here on.

front: 07 Control Abstraction > Control Abstraction > What happens to the variable inside of a function using call by result after the function is done running?
back: It is destroyed (removed from the stack)

front: 07 Control Abstraction > Control Abstraction > What is call by value/result?
back: Like call by reference, but it copies the variable from outside the function to the variable inside the function and then returns when done.

front: 07 Control Abstraction > Control Abstraction > What happens to the variable inside of a function using call by result/value after the function is done running?
back: It is destroyed (removed from the stack)

front: 07 Control Abstraction > Control Abstraction > What is the difference between call by name and call by need?
back: Call by name is evaluated every time it is used, while call by need is evaluated the first time it is used (Haskell has need)

front: 07 Control Abstraction > Control Abstraction > What is call by name?
back: In call by name the variable is evaluated every time it is used.

front: 07 Control Abstraction > Control Abstraction > Is deep binding used with static or dynamic scope?
back: Static

front: 07 Control Abstraction > Control Abstraction > What is the difference between deep binding and shallow binding?
back: Deep binding binds the environment at the time a procedure is passed as an argument. Shallow binding binds the environment at the time a procedure is actually called. See more: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/15550648/shallow-deep-binding-what-would-this-program-print

front: 07 Control Abstraction > Control Abstraction > What is an exception?
back: An event which occurs during the execution of the program, that disrupts the normal flow of the programs instructions.
